factors.summary: Demand trends are impacted by seasonal patterns and weather conditions, with slower winter months and increased demand during severe weather.

evidence.text: Generally, business is slower in the winter months versus the warmer months of the year, resulting in lower productivity. Severe weather can increase demand for electrical service and repair work.

sentiment.label: neutral
sentiment.rationale: Demand trends are influenced by seasonal and weather factors, creating mixed signals.

factors.summary: Pricing power varies between service types, with installation work typically having higher margins than maintenance work.

evidence.text: Installation work is often performed on a fixed price basis, while maintenance work is often performed under pre-established or negotiated prices or cost-plus pricing arrangements. Margins for installation work may vary from project to project, and may be higher than maintenance work.

sentiment.label: positive
sentiment.rationale: Higher margins in installation work indicate a positive trend.

factors.summary: Revenue mix is shifting towards higher-margin segments, particularly in certain geographies and industries.

evidence.text: The mix of business conducted in the areas we serve will also affect margins, as some of the areas we serve offer the opportunity for higher margins than others due to the geographic characteristics associated with the physical location where the work is being performed.

sentiment.label: positive
sentiment.rationale: Shift towards higher-margin segments suggests positive development.

factors.summary: The oil and gas infrastructure services segment is experiencing growth due to increased customer spending.

evidence.text: Revenues from oil and gas infrastructure services increased $190.6 million, or 7.8%, partially offset by a decrease in electric power infrastructure services revenues.

sentiment.label: positive
sentiment.rationale: Growth in oil and gas infrastructure services indicates positive momentum.

factors.summary: Gross margins are compressing due to lower revenues from high-margin projects and unfavorable weather conditions.

evidence.text: Gross profit as a percentage of revenues decreased to 12.2% for the year ended December 31, 2015 from 15.1% for the year ended December 31, 2014. This decrease was primarily due to the decrease in revenues from large electric transmission and mainline transmission projects, which typically yield higher margins.

sentiment.label: negative
sentiment.rationale: Decreasing gross margins due to lower revenues and weather impact.

factors.summary: Near-term risks include foreign currency fluctuations and weather impacts on margins.

evidence.text: Fluctuations in exchange rates relative to the U.S. dollar, primarily the Canadian and Australian dollars, could cause material fluctuations in comparisons of our results of operations between periods. Adverse or favorable weather conditions can impact gross margins in a given period.

sentiment.label: negative
sentiment.rationale: Foreign currency and weather risks pose potential negative impacts.

factors.summary: Key sensitivities include foreign currency exchange rates and weather conditions, which can significantly impact margins and results.

evidence.text: Fluctuations in exchange rates relative to the U.S. dollar, primarily the Canadian and Australian dollars, could cause material fluctuations in comparisons of our results of operations between periods. Adverse or favorable weather conditions can impact gross margins in a given period.

sentiment.label: negative
sentiment.rationale: Sensitivities to foreign exchange and weather may negatively impact margins.

factors.summary: Construction activity and infrastructure spending are driving demand, particularly in the oil and gas sector.

evidence.text: Revenues from oil and gas infrastructure services increased $190.6 million, or 7.8%, partially offset by a decrease in electric power infrastructure services revenues.

sentiment.label: positive
sentiment.rationale: Construction activity and infrastructure spending growth in oil and gas.
